skill.name: design-an-interface
skill.description: Generate several distinct interface designs for a software module, compare their trade-offs, and synthesize the strongest option.
skill.summary: Explore multiple API or module interface designs before choosing an implementation direction.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged one external command pattern and many weak cryptography patterns, but review found only Markdown prose and a fenced prompt template in SKILL.md. No executable scripts, command invocations, cryptographic code, network access, filesystem access, or prompt injection attempts were found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Fenced Prompt Template Misread as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static external command finding points to a Markdown code fence containing a prompt template for sub-agents. It is instructional text, not Ruby backtick execution or a runnable shell command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are inside a Markdown fence labeled as a prompt template. No shell command, Ruby code, or execution instruction appears in that block.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alerts Match Natural Language
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts occur throughout ordinary Markdown guidance about interface design. No cryptographic API, hashing function, encryption routine, or insecure algorithm implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the full file shows a prose workflow for API design. The matched lines contain headings, checklist items, and design criteria rather than cryptographic code.

content.value_statement: Teams often accept the first API shape before comparing alternatives. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code to generate distinct designs, explain trade-offs, and synthesize a stronger interface.
actual_capabilities: Gathers module requirements before proposing an interface.
Creates three or more distinct interface design directions.
Assigns different constraints to each design exploration.
Presents interface signatures, usage examples, and hidden complexity.
Compares designs using simplicity, flexibility, efficiency, depth, and misuse risk.
Helps synthesize a final design from the strongest ideas.
limitations: It does not implement the selected interface.
It depends on the user to provide accurate module requirements.
It does not benchmark runtime performance or memory usage.
It may need adaptation for strict framework or language conventions.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Library API
use_cases.description: Compare several public API shapes before committing to a package interface.

use_cases.title: Refactor a Module Boundary
use_cases.description: Explore simpler module interfaces that hide implementation details and reduce caller burden.

use_cases.title: Review Architecture Options
use_cases.description: Use structured alternatives to discuss trade-offs during technical design review.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Module Design
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you know the module purpose but not the interface shape.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use design-an-interface for a module that does [describe purpose]. The callers are [describe callers]. Generate three different interface options and explain the trade-offs.

prompt_templates.title: API Surface Comparison
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when choosing between concise and flexible APIs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use design-an-interface to compare API designs for [module]. Include one minimal design, one flexible design, and one optimized for the common case.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Existing Interface
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when an existing module feels too complex or shallow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use design-an-interface to redesign this existing interface: [describe current methods]. Focus on hiding internal complexity and making correct usage easier.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Architecture Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when a design must satisfy multiple constraints.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use design-an-interface for [module] with these constraints: [performance, compatibility, migration, testing]. Generate four radically different designs, then synthesize a recommended interface.

output_examples.input: Design an interface for a feature flag module used by web handlers and background jobs.
output: A minimal lookup design with one evaluation method.
A context-rich design that supports targeting and audit metadata.
A batch-oriented design for efficient background processing.
A prose comparison that recommends the best fit for the primary caller.

output_examples.input: Redesign a cache wrapper that currently exposes storage internals to callers.
output: Several interface options that hide serialization and eviction details.
Usage examples showing how callers read, write, and invalidate values.
A trade-off discussion about simplicity, flexibility, and misuse risk.

best_practices: Provide the module purpose, callers, core operations, and constraints before asking for designs.
Ask for designs that optimize for different goals, such as simplicity, flexibility, or common-case speed.
Review usage examples closely because they reveal caller friction better than signatures alone.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for only one design when the goal is interface exploration.
Do not skip trade-off comparison after alternatives are generated.
Do not use the skill as an implementation generator without a separate coding step.

faq.question: What does this skill help design?
faq.answer: It helps design module interfaces, API surfaces, method shapes, parameters, and caller workflows.

faq.question: Does it write production code?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on interface shape, usage examples, and trade-off analysis before implementation.

faq.question: Why generate multiple designs?
faq.answer: Multiple designs make trade-offs visible and reduce the chance of accepting a weak first idea.

faq.question: Can it use sub-agents?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow asks for parallel sub-agents when the host tool supports that capability.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide the module purpose, intended callers, key operations, constraints, and what complexity should stay hidden.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The reviewed file contains only Markdown guidance and no executable code or malicious instructions.
